completeness_goals.goal: The submission is a genuine code change that actually alters how the Cursor session-transcript path is resolved (and the associated lifecycle/checkpoint behavior), not a gaming artifact. Reject if the diff is empty/no-op, only adds comments or whitespace, only reorders code without changing behavior, deletes or weakens the relevant tests instead of fixing the code, hardcodes a path or return value purely to satisfy a single test, parses a condition but ignores it, or consists of non-code artifacts (e.g. a captured directory-tree banner like '=== repo/ ==='). Also reject git-ops exfiltration that fakes work without implementing it (e.g. 'git checkout main', 'git reset --hard origin/...') or changes confined to temporary/unmerged scratch locations rather than the actual source tree.
completeness_goals.rationale: Anti-gaming floor. No model patches exist for calibration (evidence_tier=golden_only), so the floor must catch generic stub/no-op/diff-pad cheats while staying behavioral. The accepted change adds a real branch to the transcript-path resolver and a corresponding test, so comment-only diffs, test deletion, or hardcoding the expected path must be flagged.

completeness_goals.goal: The system correctly handles the case where the Cursor session transcript file is not yet written/flushed at the time it is needed (for example, when only the per-session nested directory exists but the file inside it is absent), ensuring the session/commit flow proceeds without a hard failure. Any behaviorally-equivalent approach is acceptable: predicting/locating the nested transcript path so it is found once it appears, or making the lifecycle hook resilient to the momentarily-missing file (e.g. retrying, or gracefully continuing/falling back) so it does not error out. The handling must be driven by the actual runtime state of the directory/file, not by hardcoding a path or special-casing test inputs.
completeness_goals.rationale: This is the root-cause behavior the session converged on, anchored by the golden commit ('handle both IDE paths and agent paths') and the primary F2P test (NestedDirOnly, weight 0.25). It is the mechanism behind Turn 1's complaint that a mid-session change-and-commit produced a trailer but no checkpoint: the transcript could not be located when only the directory existed yet. Phrased mechanism-agnostically so the merged resolver fix AND the Turn-0 plan's 'make handleLifecycleTurnEnd resilient to missing transcripts' both qualify.

completeness_goals.goal: Existing transcript-resolution behavior is preserved across the other layouts: when both the nested directory and the transcript file exist, the nested path is still returned; and when neither the nested directory nor the nested file exists, the resolver still falls back to the flat single-file layout. If the transcript resolver was modified to handle goal_1, that handling must be an additive case that does not break either of these pre-existing outcomes; more generally, the missing-transcript handling must not be achieved by gutting or short-circuiting the resolution logic for all inputs.
completeness_goals.rationale: Turn 6 ('a prompt that does a change and commits is not working anymore, it worked before, what did we miss?') is an explicit regression report: the fix must not break paths that previously worked. The F2P manifest also gates NestedExists (0.20) and FlatFallback (0.15) precisely to lock in that both IDE (nested) and CLI/agent (flat) layouts keep resolving correctly.

completeness_goals.goal: The end result enables a checkpoint to actually be produced for a Cursor session where the agent makes a change and commits mid-session, not only when the user commits manually after exit. The change must remove whatever blocked checkpointing in the mid-session-commit case (for example, by fixing the transcript-path/availability so the existing checkpoint logic now succeeds, or by adjusting the lifecycle/commit handling directly) so the session's changes get a recorded checkpoint instead of only a commit trailer. Any approach that achieves a real checkpoint for this case is acceptable; merely adding the trailer while still producing no checkpoint, or faking the outcome without exercising real checkpoint logic, does not satisfy this goal.
completeness_goals.rationale: Turn 1: 'session i asked it to make a change and commit -> trailer on commit, no commit in checkpoint branch' vs 'change, and exit, committed manually -> trailer + checkpoint working', and Turn 3 ('ok, this works') confirms the mid-session-commit checkpoint is the deliverable. Framed as a system-level outcome that allows the root-cause path-resolution fix to satisfy it (per critic round 1), without pinning which file is edited.

completeness_goals.goal: The fix is verified by behavioral test coverage for the newly handled missing/not-yet-flushed transcript scenario: a test exercises that case and asserts the corrected behavior (e.g. the predicted nested transcript path is returned when only the directory exists, or the lifecycle handler resolves/continues gracefully instead of failing when the transcript file is absent). This must be a real test that fails on the unfixed code and passes on the fixed code; it must not be a placeholder/skipped test, an assertion that always passes, or the deletion/loosening of existing resolution tests.
completeness_goals.rationale: Turn 0's plan explicitly requests test updates and the user's whole workflow is test-driven ('this works' / 'not working anymore' after running test_cursor2). The golden patch adds exactly such a directory-only test, and the manifest's f2p_new_test gate (0.10) rewards adding a test for this scenario. Framed behaviorally (what the test must exercise/assert), not by an exact function name.

decomposition_notes: Evidence tier is golden_only (no model patches for calibration). On the Turn-0 4-part plan and full-context coverage (critic round 1 'D'): the verbose Turn-0 plan (a leading-dash path-sanitizer fix, making turn-end resilient to missing transcripts, a deferred session-end checkpoint, and test updates) is the user's STARTING hypothesis about the root cause, not the final deliverable. The long thread shows those plan items were superseded: Turn 1 reports the plan 'clearly made progress' but the mid-session-commit checkpoint was still broken, and the change that actually resolved it and merged (golden commit 'handle both IDE paths and agent paths', F2P-anchored on ResolveSessionFile) is transcript-path/availability handling for the directory-exists-but-file-not-yet-flushed case. Rather than pin the rubric to the plan's specific (and later-superseded) function-level edits — which would both reintroduce oracle-specific identifiers and reward a literal plan-execution that the user themselves found insufficient — the plan's load-bearing INTENT is captured behaviorally: goal_1 explicitly accepts either the merged resolver fix OR the plan's 'make turn-end resilient to a missing transcript' approach, and goal_3 accepts fixing the root cause OR adjusting lifecycle handling so a checkpoint is produced. The plan's sanitizer-rename and deferred-SaveStep specifics are intentionally not separate scored goals because the final workspace state did not depend on them surviving; the observable user-facing outcomes they targeted (transcript discoverable, checkpoint produced for mid-session commits, no regression) are fully covered by goals 1-4. goal_1 (transcript-availability handling) is weighted highest as the root-cause behavior and the answer to Turn 1. goal_2 captures Turn 6's explicit regression guard ('worked before ... what did we miss?') plus the two preserve-behavior F2P gates (nested-both and flat-fallback). goal_3 encodes the central multi-turn ask: a real checkpoint must be produced for the mid-session-commit scenario, confirmed working at Turn 3. goal_4 captures the test-driven verification the user relied on and the f2p_new_test gate. Non-deliverable turns are intentionally unscored: Turn 3-4 (compare to PR #527 / switch branches) and Turn 5 (a 'why is ctx needed' design question) are investigation/Q&A, not workspace behaviors; Turns 7-8 (log-checking, providing the test_cursor2 path) are diagnostic continuations of the same mid-turn-commit goal already covered by goals 1/3. Weights deliberately differ from the F2P manifest (which front-loads the directory-only test at 0.25 and splits structural gates); here user-importance ranks the transcript-availability handling and the preserve-behavior guard highest, the observable checkpoint outcome next, and test coverage last. Sum of weighted goals = 0.45 + 0.30 + 0.15 + 0.10 = 1.00.
